coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric nanoplatform for fluorimetric and visual detection of Cu2+ and biothiols based on Eu MOFs and carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Tao-Li
Dong, Xin-Xin
Kong, Xiang-Juan
Mao, Zhi-Jie
Kong, Fang-Fang
Chen, Shuai-Hua
Xiao, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a ratiometric nanoplatform was established for fluorimetric and visual detection of Cu2+ and biothiols based on europium-based metal–organic frameworks (Eu MOFs) and carbon dots (CDs). Fluorescent Eu MOFs with red emission was facilely prepared via the coordination of Eu3+ with tetracycline (TC) and 2-[4-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazin-1-yl] ethanesulfonic acid (Hepes). However, with addition of Cu2+ , the fluorescence of Eu MOFs significantly decreased due to higher affinity of Cu2+ with TC, whereas the green fluorescence of CDs was not influenced by Cu2+, which served as an internal standard of the sensing platform. Interestingly, after introducing biothiols, the stronger coordination between Cu2+ and the sulfydryl of biothiols induced the release of TC to coordinate with Eu3+, thus recovering the fluorescence of Eu MOFs. The ratio changes of these two emissions accompanying by the distinct color variations of the solution under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp could be used as output signals for fluorimetric and visual detection of Cu2+ and biothiols. The nanoplatform exhibited excellent sensing performance with limit detection as low as 0.57 μM for Cu2+, 0.54 μM for glutathione, 0.14 μM for homocyeteine and 0.42 µM for cysteine, respectively. In addition, it was successfully applied to detecting Cu2+ in environment water samples and biothiols in human serum samples, suggesting great potential for Cu2+ and biothiols determination in environmental and biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric nanoplatform
Fluorimetric and visual detection
Cu2+
Biothiols
Eu MOFs
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cu2+ with the ratiometric nanoplatform
Synthesis and characterization of the Eu MOFs and CDs
Feasibility of the ratiometric nanoplatform for Cu2+ and biothiols detection
Quantitation of Cu2+ and biothiols by fluorescence spectrophotometer
Determination of Cu2+ and biothiols by smartphone
Sensitivity of the ratiometric nanoplatform toward Cu2+ and biothiols
Application of the ratiometric nanoplatform for Cu2+ and biothiols detection in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Excitation and emission spectra of Eu MOFs. The insets were the photographs of Eu MOFs under natural light and UV-lamp irradiation of 365 nm. (B) TEM image, (C) the corresponding size distribution, (D) XPS survey spectrum, (E) Eu3d and (F) Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) image of Eu MOFs.

(A) Excitation and emission spectra of CDs. The insets were the photographs of CDs under natural light and UV-lamp irradiation of 365 nm. (B) TEM image, (C) the corresponding size distribution and (D) XPS survey spectrum of CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectral responses of the ratiometric nanoplatform to different mixtures. The insets were the photographs of the samples under a 365 nm UV-lamp irradiation: tube a, Hepes + Eu3+ +TC+CDs; tube b, Hepes + Cu2+ +Eu3+ +TC+CDs; tube c, Hepes + GSH+Eu3+ +TC+CDs; tube d, Hepes + GSH+Cu2+ + Eu3+ +TC+CDs. (B) Fluorescence excitation spectrum and (C) UV-vis absorbance spectrum of Eu MOFs with and without Cu2+. (D) TEM image of the Eu MOFs with Cu2+. (E) Fluorescence life time of Eu MOFs with and without Cu2+.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the sensing system toward Cu2+ with various concentrations. (B) Plots of the fluorescence ratio of I494/I615 versus Cu2+ concentrations. The insets were linear relationships between the concentrations of Cu2+ (0–40 μM and 50–100 μΜ). (C) CIE chromaticity coordinates acquired from the fluorescence emission spectra shown in (A). (D) Plots of the ratio of G/R versus Cu2+ concentrations. The inset in (D) was linear relationship between the concentration of Cu2+ (0–40 μM) and the ratio of G/R. Error bars represent standard deviation of three repetitive experiments.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the sensing system toward GSH with various concentrations. (B) Plots of the intensity ratio of I615/I494 versus GSH concentrations. (C) CIE chromaticity coordinates acquired from the fluorescence emission spectra shown in (A). (D) Plots of the ratio of R/G versus various GSH concentrations. Error bars represent standard deviation of three repetitive experiments.

Selectivity of the ratiometric nanoplatform toward Cu2+. (A) Fluorescence emission spectra, (B) the fluorescence ratio of I494/I615, (C) optical images and (D) the ratio of G/R in the presence of Cu2+ and interfering substances. The concentrations of Cu2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ni2+ and Tb3+ were 80 μM, the concentrations of other ions were 400 μM. Error bars represent standard deviation of three repetitive experiments.

Selectivity of the ratiometric nanoplatform toward biothiols. (A) Fluorescence emission spectra, (B) the intensity ratio of I615/I494, (C) optical images, (D) the ratio of R/G in the presence of GSH, Hcy, Cys and interfering substances. The concentrations of these molecules were 200 μM. Error bars represent standard deviation of three repetitive experiments.
The illustration of the ratiometric nanoplatform for fluorimetric (A) and visual (B) detection of Cu2+ and biothiols based on Eu MOFs and CDs.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric nanoplatform for fluorimetric and visual detection of Cu2+ and biothiols based on Eu MOFs and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules, Institute of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules
Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules, Institute of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330013, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The illustration of the ratiometric nanoplatform for fluorimetric (A) and visual (B) detection of Cu2+ and biothiols based on Eu MOFs and CDs.

Herein, a ratiometric nanoplatform was established for fluorimetric and visual detection of Cu2+ and biothiols based on europium-based metal–organic frameworks (Eu MOFs) and carbon dots (CDs). Fluorescent Eu MOFs with red emission was facilely prepared via the coordination of Eu3+ with tetracycline (TC) and 2-[4-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazin-1-yl] ethanesulfonic acid (Hepes). However, with addition of Cu2+ , the fluorescence of Eu MOFs significantly decreased due to higher affinity of Cu2+ with TC, whereas the green fluorescence of CDs was not influenced by Cu2+, which served as an internal standard of the sensing platform. Interestingly, after introducing biothiols, the stronger coordination between Cu2+ and the sulfydryl of biothiols induced the release of TC to coordinate with Eu3+, thus recovering the fluorescence of Eu MOFs. The ratio changes of these two emissions accompanying by the distinct color variations of the solution under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp could be used as output signals for fluorimetric and visual detection of Cu2+ and biothiols. The nanoplatform exhibited excellent sensing performance with limit detection as low as 0.57 μM for Cu2+, 0.54 μM for glutathione, 0.14 μM for homocyeteine and 0.42 µM for cysteine, respectively. In addition, it was successfully applied to detecting Cu2+ in environment water samples and biothiols in human serum samples, suggesting great potential for Cu2+ and biothiols determination in environmental and biological samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper (II) ions (Cu2+) is an essential element for human life activities, which is physiologically significant in various aspects such as bone formation, cellular respiration, and serving as a significant catalytic cofactor for several metalloenzymes [1]. However, both high and low content of copper might affect human health. High Cu2+ levels can cause damage to central nervous system and disorders associated with neurodegenerative diseases, while Cu2+ deficiency is associated with hematological abnormalities, kidney diseases and cardiovascular diseases [2,3]. Biothiols, including glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy) and cysteine (Cys), play a crucial role in biological processes in organisms and the abnormal levels of them are closely related to many diseases. For instance, the level of GSH in the cellular is associated with leucocyte loss and HIV infection [4,5]. Excessive level of Hcy in living system is in connection with Alzheimer's disease, osteoporosis and cardiovascular disease [6,7]. The deficiency of Cys may lead to slow growth, lethargy, hair fading, skin diseases, liver damage and edema [8]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop efficient strategies for highly sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ and biothiols.

Up to now, a variety of instrumental techniques have been used to quantify the concentrations of Cu2+ and biothiols including atomic absorption spectrometry, high-performance liquid chromatography and inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy [9–11]. Although these methods have been demonstrated with good accuracy, they are quite complex, expensive and often requires sophisticated pretreatment as well as trained expertise. Thus, developing methods with simple operation, low-cost and high sensitivity for Cu2+ and biothiols detection is a matter of interest. In recent years, fluorescence analysis methods have attracted great interest of many researchers due to their high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and simple operation [12]. However, most of the fluorescence sensors give results according to emission data at a single wavelength, the accuracy of which is prone to be affected by external factors including inhomogeneities in probe concentration, fluctuation of excitation power and instrumental efficiency [13–15]. Ratiometric fluorescence sensors can eliminate these environmental effects and improve the sensitivity and reliability by taking advantage of the self-calibrated reference fluorescence signal.

Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), which are constructed from metal ions or metal ions clusters and organic ligands by coordination bonds, have emerged as one of the research hotspots owing to their promising applications in the fields of gas storage/separation [16,17], magnetocaloric effect [18,19], information storage [20,21], sensing [22–24] and heterogeneous catalysis [25,26]. Lanthanide metal–organic frameworks (Ln MOFs), as a promising subfamily of MOFs, have been widely used for developing fluorescent sensors because of their unique properties, such as undisturbed emissive energy, bright visible emission and large stokes shift, particularly those with terbium and europium as metal nodes [27–29]. Up to now, Ln MOFs have been reported to detect various species including anions [30], metal ions [31,32], pH [33,34], aromatic explosives [35], biomarkers [36], temperature [37] and so on. However, Ln MOFs sensors for sequential detection of Cu2+ and biothiols have rarely been reported.

Smartphone-based detection devices have attracted much attention in recent years due to their relatively portable and low cost, increasing the suitability of data acquisition and processing power. The key to the successful applications of smartphones were ascribed to the built-in high-resolution camera and the open source app which can capture the digital imaging of the analytes and read out the tricolor information of red (R), yellow (G), and blue (B) values to display the results of analyte concentrations. Very recently, intergrating ratiometric sensors with new smartphones-based detection technology has been proven to be a promising strategy for visual sensing of pH [38], ions [39] and biomarkers [40].

Inspired by the above-mentioned facts, herein, we developed a novel ratiometric nanoplatform for fluorimetric and visual detection of Cu2+ and biothiols using europium-based MOFs (Eu MOFs) with red emission at 615 nm and carbon dots (CDs) with blue fluorescence at 494 nm as output signals. It was found that fluorescent Eu MOFs could be facilely synthesized via coordination of Eu3+ with TC and Hepes. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , with introduction of Cu2+, the generation of fluorescent Eu MOFs was effectively blocked because of strong coordination between Cu2+ and TC, causing the fluorescence decrease at 615 nm, while the fluorescence of CDs at 494 nm played a reference role with undetectable changes. Contrastingly, when biothiols was present, Cu2+ preferentially bound to the sulfhydryl group of thiols, resulting in in-situ regeneration of Eu MOFs and fluorescence recovery at 615 nm. Thus, the distinct changes in fluorescence ratio of Eu MOFs to CDs accompanying by obvious solution color changed from red to green enabled the establishment of a ratiometric sensor for fluorometric and visual detection of Cu2+ and biothiols. The proposed platform exhibited good sensing performances with limit detection as low as 0.57 μM for Cu2+, 0.54 μM for GSH, 0.14 μM for Hcy and 0.42 μM for Cys, respectively. In addition, it was successfully applied to Cu2+ detection in environmental water (lake water and tap water) and biothiols in human serum samples, suggesting great potential for detecting Cu2+ and biothiols in environmental and biological samples.

Tetracycline (TC), 2-[4-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazin-1-yl] ethanesulfonic acid (Hepes), glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy), cysteine (Cys), ascorbic acid (AA), tyrosine (Tyr), D-proline (Pro), arginine (Arg), glycine (Gly) and alanine (Ala) were acquired from Sangon Biotechnology Company (Shanghai, China). NaIO4 and p-phenylenediamines were purchased from Energy Chemical (China). All chemicals were of analytical grade. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm).

Fluorescence spectra were carried out with a 96-well black microplate on a Varioskan LUX microplate reader with 365 nm excitation light (Thermo Scientific, USA) and absorption spectra were determined on an 8453 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using TECNAI G2 20. Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR) spectrum was recorded with Spectrum Two spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA).

CDs were prepared by a modified solvothermal method [41]. In a typical synthesis, 30 mg p-phenylenediamines and 120 mg NaIO4 were dissolved in 30 mL ethanol. Then the solution was transferred into an 80 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Subsequently, the autoclave was heated up to 180℃ for 1.5 h. The reaction mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature. After that, the purification of CDs was proceeded with silica gel column chromatography using a mixture of dichloromethane and ethanol (VDichloromethane:VEthanol = 50:1) as eluents for 2 h, the purified CDs were redispersed in DMF and stored at 4℃ prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ with the ratiometric nanoplatform
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 5 μL Hepes buffer (500 mM, pH=7.0), various concentrations of Cu2+ and appropriate sterilized water were mixed and incubated at room temperature for 2 min, respectively. Thereafter, 5 μL Eu3+ (1 mM) and 15 μL TC (1 mM) were added to the prepared solution and incubated for 2 min at room temperature. 90 μL of each sample was used for fluorescence measurements after adding 2.5 μL CDs and incubating at room temperature for 10 min. The fluorescence signals were detected with excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The color changes were captured by the camera of a smartphone under a 365 nm UV lamp and processed by an open source of Color Detection APP.

Firstly, 10 μL varying concentration of biothiols was added into 67.5 μL solution containing 1 μL Cu2+ (10 mM), 5 μL Hepes buffer (500 mM, pH=7.0) and 61.5 μL sterilized water, the reaction was proceeded at room temperature for 10 min. Then, 5 μL Eu3+ (1 mM) and 15 μL TC (1 mM) were added to the prepared solution and incubated for 2 min at room temperature. After adding 2.5 μL CDs, the solution was mixed and incubated at room temperature for 10 min, and then, 90 μL of the sample was subjected to fluorescence measurement. The emission spectra were recorded with excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The color changes were captured by the camera of a smartphone under 365 nm UV lamp and processed by an open source of Color Detection APP.

The environmental water samples were obtained from Weilai Lake in our campus and tap water in our lab respectively. The water samples were firstly filtered three times using 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove the solid suspensions and followed by boiling and cooling. The subsequent detection procedures were carried out as same as those described above.

The standard addition method was employed to evaluate the reliability of the sensor for biothiols in human serum. Normal human serum sample was firstly centrifuged at 16,000 r/min for 20 min, and followed by filtering with 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove particulate matter in the obtained supernatant. Different amounts of biothiols were spiked into the purified supernatant for fluorescence detection as mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the Eu MOFs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Being an ideal ligand of Eu3+, TC has carbonyl and carboxyl groups, which can coordinate with Eu3+. It was interestingly to note that the mixture solution of Eu3+, TC and Hepes could trigger in situ generation of Eu MOFs, which displayed a characteristic fluorescence maximum emission at 615 nm with excitation of 399 nm, and the strong red fluorescence was visible to naked eye upon UV illumination at 365 nm (Fig. 1 A). By contrast, when one the of components, TC, Hepes or Eu3+, essential for the formation of Eu MOFs, was withdrawn from the system, an extremely weak fluorescence at 615 nm was observed. The significant enhancement of Eu3+ emission should be ascribed to “antenna effect”, in which the energy absorbed by TC was effectively transferred to Eu3+ [42]. The morphology of Eu MOFs generated in the system were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1B, TEM image revealed that the generated Eu MOFs were spherical, and the sizes were mostly distributed ranging from 15 to 60 nm with average size of 37.32 nm in diameter (Fig. 1C). XPS measurement of Eu MOFs in Fig. 1D displayed the element signals of Eu, C, N, O and S. The elements except Eu were corresponding to the compositions of TC and Hepes (Fig. S1). The Eu 3d spectrum indicated two peaks at 1133.6 eV and 1163.6 eV (Fig. 1E), which were attributed to Eu 3d5 and Eu 3d3, respectively. The high-resolution XPS peak images of C1s, N 1s, O1s and S2p in Fig. S2 reflecting the bonding structures on the formed Eu MOF. As shown in Fig. S2A, the C1s spectrum showed four distinctive peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.2 eV, 287.1 eV and 288.5 eV, which were assigned to CC/ C–C, C–N/–C–S, C–O/C–O–C and CO groups, respectively. In Fig. S2B, three characteristic peaks at 398.5 eV and 401.3 eV in N1s spectrum related to the presence of C-N and N-H groups, respectively. In Fig. S2C, two peaks centred at 531.68 eV and 533.23 eV were decomposed in O1s spectrum, which indicated CO and C-OH groups, respectively. As for the S2p spectrum (Fig. S2D), the peak at 164.1 eV was attributed to the groups of-CS bond. The fitted peaks of S2p at 167.6 eV and 168.5 eV was belonged to the typical characteristic group of –SO3 − group [43–45]. The PXRD pattern of the obtained Eu MOFs in Fig. 1F indicated high crystallinity of the product.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with green-emitting were rapidly synthesized by solvothermal method of treating p-phenylenediamine and NaIO4 in ethanol solution. The prepared CDs showed an emission peak at 494 nm with excitation wavelength of 361 nm, and a bright green fluorescence was observed under a 365 nm UV lamp (the inset image in Fig. 2 A). As shown in Fig. 2B-C, the uniformly distributed CDs were spherical and the size was relatively small ranging from 1.0 to 6.0 nm with average diameter of 3.25 nm. XPS was further employed to determine the surface composition of CDs. The full XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 2D showed the signals of C, N and O elements, and the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N 1s and O1s were shown in Fig. S3. In the C1s spectrum (Fig. S3A), three peaks at 284.2 eV, 285.6 eV, and 288.5 eV were attributed to the chemical bonds of C–C/CC, C-N/C-O, and CO, respectively. The N1s spectrum displayed two peaks at 398.1 eV, and 399.4 eV (Fig. S3B), which confirmed the bonds of pyrrolic N and pyridine N, respectively [41]. The O1s spectrum confirmed three bands at 531.1 eV, 531.9 eV and 532.5 eV (Fig. S3C), which confirmed the bonds of C–O, CO and C–OH, respectively.
The above results demonstrated the successful preparation of Eu MOFs and CDs.

As shown in Fig. S4, it was noteworthy that when Eu MOFs and CDs were excited with wavelength from 349 to 429 nm and from 321 to 401 nm, respectively, the fluorescence emission wavelengths of these two nanomaterials were unchanged, indicating the uniformity size and surface state of these particles. The stability of the prepared Eu MOFs and CDs were also tested. As shown in Fig. S5 and Fig. S6, no obvious fluorescence decrease of Eu MOFs and CDs could be observed when the fluorescent particles were treated with different conditions including various pH (6.8–8.2), placed at room temperature within different days and under continuous irradiation with 399 nm light excitation for Eu MOFs and 361 nm light excitation for CDs, revealing excellent stability of these two nanomaterials and great potential for analysis application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the ratiometric nanoplatform for Cu2+ and biothiols detection
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the feasibility of the nanoplatform, fluorescence spectra measurements were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 3 A and Fig. S7, upon excitation with 365 nm UV light, coexistence of TC and Eu3+ with CDs in Hepes buffer exhibited strong fluorescence peaks at 494 nm and 615 nm respectively, which corresponded to CDs and the in-situ formed Eu MOFs. After addition of Cu2+, the fluorescence of Eu MOFs at 615 nm dramatically decreased while the fluorescence of CDs at 494 nm was almost unchanged, and the color of the solution changed from yellow to green as well. In contrast, with addition of biothiols only, there was no remarkable fluorescence changes, which verified that there was negligible interference from biothiols individually. However, when Cu2+ and biothiols were mixed, the fluorescence at 615 nm was obviously increased accompanying by the solution color varied from green to yellow. These results verified the feasibility of the developed ratiometric nanoplatform for Cu2+ and biothiols detection.

To confirm the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Eu MOFs caused by Cu2+, excitation and absorption spectra of Eu MOFs before and after addition of Cu2+ were firstly investigated. As shown in Fig. 3B-C, upon addition of Cu2+, the excitation peak band maximum at about 399 nm disappeared and the absorption maximum band increased accompanied by a slight shift, demonstrating that Cu2+ disturbed the formation of Eu MOFs. This observation was further demonstrated and characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 3D, with addition of Cu2+, no Eu MOFs could be observed, instead, only few smaller nanoparticles appeared, the fact might be probably due to that Cu2+ preferentially binds to TC with the higher stability and efficiently competes with the interaction of Eu3+ and TC, inhibiting the formation of Eu MOFs. Fluorescence lifetime measurements with different components were further employed to explore fluorescence quenching mechanism of Eu MOFs caused by Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 3E, it was noticed that the fluorescence lifetime of in-situ obtained Eu MOFs was estimated to be 16.01 µs and the addition of Cu2+ made the fluorescence lifetime decreased to 15.24 µs, suggesting that the energy loss of the triplet state of Eu3+ was affected by Cu2+. From the above results, it could be concluded that strong coordination of Cu2+ and TC weakened the energy transfer between the TC and Eu3+, which should be responsible for the fluorescence decrease of Eu MOFs.

To obtain excellent analytical performance for Cu2+ and biothiols detection, the experimental conditions were systematically optimized. The setting of excitation wavelength for the detection platform was firstly explored. Taking the emission intensities of Eu MOFs and CDs under different excitation wavelengths and the parameter setting limits of the microplate reader into consideration, excitation wavelength of 365 nm was chosen for following experiments. Subsequently, the influences of the solution pH, temperature and reaction time were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S8, the optimized fluorescence intensity ratio of I494/I615 was obtained with the solution of pH 7.0 and the temperature at 25℃, and the ratio of I494/I615 reached maximum at 2 min. Therefore, the solution of pH 7.0, the temperature at 25℃ and 2 min of reaction time were selected for Cu2+ detection. Similarly, as indicated in Fig. S9, Fig. S10 and Fig. S11, the optimized ratio of I615/I494 was obtained when the solution pH achieved 7.5 as well as the temperature at 25℃, and the ratio reached maximum when the incubation time of Cu2+ and biothiols was 10 min. Therefore, the solution of pH 7.5, the temperature at 25℃ and 10 min of incubation time were selected for biothiols detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitation of Cu2+ and biothiols by fluorescence spectrophotometer
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, fluorescence measurements for Cu2+ and biothiols detection were performed. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with the concentration of Cu2+ increasing, the fluorescence of Eu MOFs at 615 nm gradually decreased while the fluorescence of CDs at 494 nm almost remained constant. The emission ratio of I494/I615 versus the concentration of Cu2+ ranging from 0 to 100 μM was displayed in Fig. 4B, two linear equations in the range of 0–40 μM and 50–100 μM were fitted: I494/I615 = 0.0251 CCu2+ + 0.5001, (R2 = 0.9943) and I494/I615 = 0.1604 CCu2+ – 6.4963 (R2 = 0.9903), respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.57 μM based on 3σ/slope. On the contrary, as shown in Fig. 5 A, Fig. S12A and Fig. S13A, with the increasing concentrations of biothiols, the fluorescence of Eu MOFs at 615 nm gradually increased. The emission ratios of I615/I494 versus the concentration of biothiols ranging from 0 to 200 μM were displayed in Fig. 5B, Fig. S12B and Fig. S13B, linear equations were fitted: I615/I494 = 0.0096 CGSH +0.0486, (R2 = 0.9901), I615/I494 = 0.0010 CHcy + 0.0321, (R2 = 0.9946) and I615/I494 = 0.0065 CCys + 0.0740, (R2 = 0.9933), respectively. The limits of detection (LODs) were calculated to be 0.54 μM for GSH, 0.14 μM for Hcy and 0.42 μM for Cys, respectively. These LODs were comparable to or much lower than those previously reported (Table S1–S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ and biothiols by smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectral assay has the advantage of excellent sensitivity, however, the naked-eye is not sensitive enough to identify the slight color change for precise onsite monitoring of Cu2+ and biothiols. To obtain images with high quality and accuracy, the samples were immediately captured by smartphone with excitation of 365 nm UV light in dark environment and identified by Color Detection APP. As shown in Fig. 4D, the ratio of G/R increased along with the concentration of Cu2+ increasing, and linear relationship with Cu2+ concentrations in the range of 0–40 μM (G/R=0.0181 CCu2+ + 0.7710, R2 = 0.9920) was displayed. The LOD was calculated to be 1.18 μM based on 3σ/slope. As shown in Fig. 5D, Fig. S12D and Fig. S13D, the ratio of R/G exhibited linear relationships with biothiols concentration: R/G=0.0051CGSH+0.2130 (R2 = 0.9893), R/G=0.0060CGSH+0.1183 (R2 = 0.9927), R/G=0.0058CHcy + 0.1487 (R2 = 0.9909), respectively. The LODs were estimated to be 2.59 μM for GSH, 2.03 μM for Hcy and 1.47 μM for Cys based on 3σ/slope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the ratiometric nanoplatform toward Cu2+ and biothiols
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is a crucial parameter for potential applications of the developed sensors, therefore, the selectivity of the ratiometric nanoplatform for Cu2+ and biothiols detection was thoroughly tested. Various metal ions including Ba2+, Fe3+, Na+, Pd2+, Mn2+, Ag+, K+, Cd2+, Sn2+, Li+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+ and Tb3+ were selected as potential interfering ions. As shown in Fig. 6 A and Fig. 6B, only Cu2+ induced a significant decrease on the fluorescence ratio of the ratiometric nanoplatform, while the other metal ions showed negligible influence. As shown in Fig. 6C and Fig. 6D, the interferences showed no obvious influence on the ratio of I494/I615 and G/R. As biothiols are important anti-oxidants in human plasma, different biomolecules including AA, Tyr, D-Pro, Arg, Gly, Ala and S2− were tested to estimate the selectivity of the ratiometric nanoplatform toward biothiols. As shown in Fig. 7 A-D, only the introduction of biothiols could result in dramatic fluorescence ratio enhancement and obvious color variation of the solution. These results suggested that the ratiometric nanoplatform owned excellent selectivity for Cu2+ and biothiols detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the ratiometric nanoplatform for Cu2+ and biothiols detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To test the practical applicability of the ratiometric nanoplatform, different amounts of Cu2+ was separately spiked into tap water and lake water samples for Cu2+ detection. The recoveries obtained by the ratio of I494/I615 and G/R in lake water and tap water were in the range of 91.10 %–106.83 % with relative standard deviations (RSD) lower than 4.14 % (Table S5 and Table S6) and 97.34 %–106.36 % with RSD lower than 4.04 % (Table S7 and Table S8), respectively.

Based on high sensitivity and excellent selectivity of the ratiometric nanoplatform for biothiols detection, its practical performance was assessed by detecting biothiols in human serum. Different amounts of 50, 100 and 150 μM were spiked to diluted serum samples, respectively. The recoveries of biothiols obtained by fluorescence ratio of I615/I494 were in the range of 90.22 %–106.97 % with RSD lower than 2.48 % (Table S9, Table S10 and Table S11). Moreover, acceptable recoveries of GSH, Hcy and Cys obtained by the ratio of R/G were in the range from 91.63 % to 105.08 % with RSD lower than 1.32 % (Table S12, Table S13 and Table S14). These satisfactory results sufficiently demonstrated that the ratiometric nanoprobe could be potentially employed for Cu2+ and biothiols detection in real samples with high accuracy.

In summary, a ratiometric nanoplatform for Cu2+ and biothiols detection was successfully designed. The nanoprobe was developed based on competitive metal ions-ligand coordination to regulate the fluorescence of Eu MOFs and employing CDs as an internal standard to improve the accuracy. This ratiometric nanoplatform has realized fluorimetric and visual dual-mode detection of Cu2+ and biothiols by fluorescence spectrophotometer and smartphone. With high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and fast response time, the ratiometric platform showed desirable applicability in determination of Cu2+ in environmental water samples and biothiols in human serum samples. The strategy is promising for multi-target sensing in environmental security assessment and biological analysis.

Tao-Li Chen: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xin-Xin Dong: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiang-Juan Kong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Zhi-Jie Mao: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Fang-Fang Kong: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Shuai-Hua Chen: Writing – review & editing. Qiang Xiao: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the NSF of Jiangxi Province (20232BAB203010), the Youth Foundation of Jiangxi Education Department (GJJ211125), the NNSF of China (No. 22261019 and Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules (No: 2024SSY05141).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111453.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
